At the beginning of a rule which gives you a brief summary of what the rule is about and these headlines they're probably the thing that most developers are familiar with because also when you are using a checker tool, then usually the warnings that you get from your checker tool, they will use the wording from those headlines.
Below the headline, we will give a category for, rule, which, is a measure for like how, how important the rule is or like how severe, the danger that is trying to protect you from.

So the, The most strict category that we have is mandatory that basically says you must always follow this rule like there's no you can't really not follow this rule this is typically reserve we only have very few rules with this category this is reserve collect really severe cases of like undefined behavior where it's absolutely clear if you write something like this then your program is broken and There's no discussions.
What is much more common is, the other 2 categories. So we have required, which says like this is, this is really like a potentially dangerous thing and in almost all cases you should not do this.
If you don't want to follow a required rule you have to deviate on a case by case basis and like give a formal.
Justification for why you can't get in trouble because of the the violation and then we have advisory, which is a little bit weaker.
So, Advisory. Has a little bit lower bar for, for deviation.
So we use it for rules where there might actually be legitimate uses where you might want to deviate from it more regularly and we want to make it easier for for you to not follow this one.

Then one of the most important sections is the rationale, because in the rationale we explain why the thing that we we ban is is bad.

I really want to write what I just wrote there, then The rationale tells you like what is potentially bad about the thing that you wrote and you now have to argue why.
